---
title: "Week 1 — Kernel, Range, Similarity, and Isomorphisms"
---

\newcommand{\R}{\mathbf{R}}

### Linear maps and matrices

Let $L:\R^{n}\to\R^{m}$ be a linear map and let $E=\{e_1,\ldots, e_n\}$ be any basis for $\R^{n}$
and $F=\{f_1,\ldots,f_{m}\}$ be any basis of $\R^{m}$.

If $x=\sum x_{i}e_{i}$ is an element of $\R^{n}$, then $L(x)$
is an element of $\R^{m}$ and so there are unique scalars 
$y_1,\ldots, y_{m}$ so that 

$$
L(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_{i}f_{i}.
$$

If we represent $x$ by the column vector
$$
x=\left[\begin{matrix} x_1 \\ \vdots \\ x_{n}\end{matrix}\right]_{E},
$$
where the subscript $E$ means that the coordinates depend on the basis $E$, then
$$
L(x) = \left[\begin{matrix} y_1 \\ \vdots \\ y_{m}\end{matrix}\right]_{F}
$$

Linearity tells us that $L$ is determined by its values $L(e_{i})$
since
$$
L(x) = \sum x_{i}L(e_{i}).
$$

Define a matrix $[L]_{E}^{F}$ whose columns are the vectors
$$
L(e_{i}) = \left[\begin{matrix}y_{1i}\\ \vdots \\ y_{mi}\end{matrix}\right].
$$

This yields an $m\times n$ matrix $[L]_{E}^{F}$ and by its construction it satisfies

$$
[L(x)]_{F} = [L]_{E}^{F}[x]_{E}
$$

This matrix $[L]$ is called the matrix of the linear map relative to the chosen bases.
